mnemonics.title: Lagrange's Theorem Core Idea
mnemonics.content: GROUP divides into equal-sized COSETS: |G| = [G:H] × |H| - Think: 'Great Groups Have Incredible Hierarchies' (G = |G|, H = |H|, I = Index)

acronyms.expansion: For finite group G: |H| divides |G| whenever H is a subgroup of G
acronyms.usage: Checking if subgroup orders are valid in a group of given order

acronyms.expansion: Set of left cosets of H in G, forms a group if and only if H is normal
acronyms.usage: Creating quotient groups for normal subgroups

acronyms.expansion: Center of G = {g ∈ G | gh = hg for all h ∈ G}
acronyms.usage: Important normal subgroup in group theory

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a rectangular cake cut into equal rectangular slices - each slice (coset) has the same number of pieces (elements), and together they form the whole cake (group G).
Think of |G| as a pizza and |H| as slices - the number of slices (index) must be a whole number, so slice size must divide pizza size.
A wheel with spokes: the group is the full wheel (360°), an element's order is how many times you rotate to get back to start - it must be a divisor of the full rotation count.

analogies.analogy: Each coset is like a parallel universe where the subgroup structure repeats, but shifted by a different 'origin' element.
Think of a subgroup as a magnifying glass - the index tells you how many different 'views' (cosets) you need to cover the entire group.
A normal subgroup is like a fair mirror - left and right reflections (cosets) give the same result, allowing a well-defined quotient.
